metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `related_ticket_ids` helper signature with `list_descendants`'s required `tracker` parameter — either add `tracker` (or `repo_root`) to the helper signature, or specify how the tracker path is resolved inside `graph.py`, and update the S7 interface contract accordingly.
findings.finding: `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` requires a `tracker` filesystem-path argument, but the proposed helper signature `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` takes only `ticket_id`. The plan never specifies how `graph.py` acquires the `tracker` path to pass to `list_descendants`, making the asserted reuse unverifiable as written.
findings.location: What section — `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` … REUSES … `descendants.list_descendants`
findings.suggested_fix: Change the helper signature to `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id: str, tracker: str) -> set[str]` (mirroring `list_descendants`), or add a `repo_root` parameter resolved via `rebar._engine_support.reads.tracker_dir()` — and document which is used in the Scope's interface contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py`'s 'CLI-only' designation reflects an import-linter or architectural boundary that blocks in-process library reuse from `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py`, and either confirm the reuse is permitted or specify that `list_descendants` should be promoted to a library-callable function.
findings.finding: The plan asserts REUSE of `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants` as a public library function, but the module's own docstring declares it 'CLI-only' and it has no existing in-process library callers — only `list_descendants_cli` calls it, invoked from the CLI entry point. The plan's characterization of this function as a 'public' reuse target conflicts with the module's stated contract.
findings.location: What section — `graph.py` … REUSES the existing public `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add coercer lambdas for every new `overlap_*` field to `_SECTIONS['verify']` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (same file, same PR as the `VerifyConfig` additions).
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as the edit target for new `overlap_*` fields added to `VerifyConfig`, but omits the required parallel update to `_SECTIONS['verify']` — the coercion-table dict in the same file that maps key names to coercer lambdas. Any key in `VerifyConfig` that is absent from `_SECTIONS['verify']` will trigger an 'unknown key' warning (or, with `strict=True`, a hard error) at load time, silently discarding the configured value.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`
findings.suggested_fix: For each new field (e.g. `overlap_k`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`, `overlap_field_weight_keywords`, etc.), add `"overlap_<name>": lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1)` (or `_as_float`, `_as_bool`, `_as_str_tuple` as appropriate) inside `_SECTIONS["verify"]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `related_ticket_ids`'s stated signature (or the plan's What section) to include how the `tracker` path is supplied to `list_descendants` — either as a parameter (`related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`) or by documenting the config-read call it will make internally.
findings.finding: `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` in `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` requires a `tracker` filesystem path as its second argument, but the plan's new `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` helper in `graph.py` has no stated parameter or mechanism for receiving the tracker path. The plan does not enumerate how `graph.py` resolves the tracker directory to pass to `list_descendants`.
findings.location: What section / `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `related_ticket_ids` signature to include the tracker path (e.g., `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id: str, tracker: str) -> set[str]`) and update the Scope and Testing sections to match, or specify that it resolves the path via `reads.tracker_dir()` and explain how the fixture overrides it in tests.
findings.finding: `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` (graph.py) must call `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` and read linked tickets (via `deps` / `build_dep_graph`), both of which require a `tracker` filesystem path. The plan's stated signature takes only `ticket_id` and provides no specification of how the helper obtains the tracker path. The plan does not say it uses `reads.tracker_dir()`, is passed a tracker argument, or reads it from config — leaving the interface incomplete for the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the stated signature to `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id: str, tracker: str) -> set[str]` and thread `tracker` through from `retrieve()`'s caller; update the fixture-graph test to pass a `tmp_path`-based tracker.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify all `overlap_*` config key names, Python types, and default values (e.g. `overlap_k: int = 20`, `overlap_field_weight_problem_keywords: float = 2.0`) in the Scope section co-located with the VerifyConfig edit target.
findings.finding: The `overlap` typed config section's key vocabulary — specific key names, Python types, and default values for field weights (problem_keywords, component_or_area, propositions, key_entities), `max_doc_freq`, stopword list, overlap floor, and `K` — is never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan only describes these as 'configurable knobs' without naming the keys or their defaults. No such section exists in `_config_schema.py` or `_SECTION_CLASSES`.
findings.location: What section / Scope section / AC5 — 'overlap typed config'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the data-flow gap in `related_ticket_ids`: specify whether the helper calls `reduce_all_tickets` directly (once, internally) to build a unified `all_states` map used for descendants, ancestors, siblings AND linked-ticket lookups — rather than delegating to `list_descendants` (which discards `all_states` from its callers) and then requiring a second scan for the sibling case.
findings.finding: `related_ticket_ids` cannot reuse `list_descendants` for sibling lookup without a second full `reduce_all_tickets` scan. `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` calls `reduce_all_tickets` internally but only returns bucketed descendant IDs — it does not expose the `all_states` map. Finding siblings (tickets sharing the same `parent_id` as the query ticket) requires iterating all ticket states to match on `parent_id`, which is data `list_descendants` discards from its callers. The plan says the helper 'REUSES list_descendants for descendants, and adds ancestors … siblings … and linked tickets' with no refactor of descendants.py — but the sibling lookup cannot be satisfied from `list_descendants`'s return value alone, so either `reduce_all_tickets` is called twice (double I/O, potentially blowing the <10ms budget) or the 'reuse' claim is structurally misleading.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids … that REUSES the existing public … list_descendants for descendants, and adds ancestors (walk parent_id up), siblings (same parent_id), and linked tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Have `related_ticket_ids` call `reduce_all_tickets` once, build its own `children_of` map and `state_by_id` map from the result, then: (a) BFS for descendants from the query ticket, (b) walk `parent_id` up for ancestors, (c) look up siblings via `children_of[query_parent_id]`, (d) collect linked ticket IDs from `state_by_id[query_id]['deps']`. This is a single I/O pass and avoids the double-scan; `list_descendants` can be retained as documentation of the BFS pattern rather than as a call-target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how related_ticket_ids obtains graph data without a full reduce_all_tickets scan: either accept a pre-loaded all_states dict as a parameter (caller loads once, passes in), or read only the query ticket's reduced state for parent_id + deps and walk ancestry with targeted lookups — and name the separate mechanism for reading the query ticket's deps[] (not covered by list_descendants).
findings.finding: list_descendants(root_id, tracker) — the function the plan names as the core reuse for graph exclusion — internally calls reduce_all_tickets(tracker), a full O(n) disk scan over the entire store. Invoking it inside the retrieval path directly contradicts the <10ms performance AC. Additionally, list_descendants covers only descendants; the query ticket's own deps (linked tickets) require a separate reduce_ticket or reduce_all_tickets call that the plan does not name. The plan presents this as a cheap, transparent reuse but the actual integration surface has both a performance incompatibility and a coverage gap.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids … that REUSES the existing public src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants'
findings.suggested_fix: Define related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, all_states: dict[str, dict]) -> set[str] — the caller (retrieve()) already loads the corpus states for scoring and can pass the same dict in. Inside: (1) walk parent_id up from all_states[ticket_id] for ancestors; (2) collect children via the existing children_of index; (3) collect siblings via same parent_id; (4) collect dep target_ids from all_states[ticket_id].get('deps', []). This eliminates the extra disk scan entirely and makes the perf AC achievable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC5 into two separate ACs: one for configurability (with `::test_knobs`) and one for error-handling (with `::test_error_handling`), each with its own named proving command.
findings.finding: AC5 is a compound acceptance criterion that bundles two distinct claims — configurability of knobs AND error-handling returning [] — into one AC with no named test method for the error-handling half.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Field weights, max_doc_freq, overlap floor, and K are configurable via the `overlap` typed config; a graph-read/scoring error yields an empty list (never blocks the caller).'
findings.suggested_fix: AC5a: 'Field weights, max_doc_freq, overlap floor, and K are configurable via the `overlap` typed config; verified by `::test_knobs`.' AC5b: 'A graph-read or scoring error yields an empty candidate list and never raises to the caller; verified by `::test_error_handling`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test reference to AC5 for the error-handling behavior (e.g., `; verified by ::test_error_handling`) so every stated guarantee has a named, checkable test.
findings.finding: AC5 bundles two distinct verifiable behaviors — (a) all knobs are configurable via `overlap` typed config, and (b) a graph-read/scoring error yields an empty list — but its verify step names no test function for (b), unlike every other AC which cites an explicit `::test_*` reference. The Testing section does name this scenario ('a graph-read error yields [] not an exception') but no AC verify step links to it, leaving the error-handling behavior effectively untested at the AC level.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5; Testing section
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC5 into two criteria, or append `; a graph-read/scoring error yields [] not an exception, verified by ::test_error_handling` to AC5's text, and add `test_error_handling` to `tests/unit/test_overlap_retrieve.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test (e.g. `test_overlap_floor_no_match`) and an AC line specifying the exact return value of `retrieve()` when no candidate meets the `minimum_should_match` floor (e.g. returns `[]` and logs a warning), so S7 can rely on a defined contract.
findings.finding: The `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor is a new behavioral contract, but the testing plan does not specify what `retrieve()` returns when NO candidates meet the floor (empty list? relaxed fallback? partial results?). This is a failure/edge path for a new user-facing function whose behavior is ambiguous and untested.
findings.location: Testing section / AC5 / `overlap` config knobs description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing section: `test_overlap_floor_no_match`: set floor=1.0 (all terms must match), use a corpus where no ticket shares all query terms, assert `retrieve()` returns `[]` and emits a log at WARNING level. Add to AC5: 'When no candidates meet the overlap floor, `retrieve()` returns `[]` and logs a WARNING.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `minimum_should_match` floor predicate precisely (e.g., 'at least N terms from `matched_terms` must appear in the candidate field'), specify the unit of the config value, and state what `retrieve()` returns when no candidates clear the floor (recommended: return [] and increment a logged floor-miss counter distinct from the stale-skip count).
findings.finding: The `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor is referenced as a configurable precision knob but its decision-rule semantics are never defined: the plan does not state (a) what the floor predicate is (minimum count of matched terms, minimum fraction, minimum BM25F score, or something else), nor (b) what `retrieve()` returns when zero candidates satisfy the floor (empty list? silently ignore floor? log a warning?). This processing protocol is not co-located with the `retrieve()`/`OverlapCandidate` contract.
findings.location: What section — 'a `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define explicit default values in the plan for ALL new `overlap_*` config knobs (field weights, `max_doc_freq`, `minimum_should_match` floor, stopword list) so that consumers running with an unmodified config file get safe, documented behavior rather than a validation error or silent misconfiguration.
findings.finding: The plan specifies K=20 as a default for the top-K knob but does not define defaults for the other configurable knobs — field weights (problem_keywords, component_or_area, propositions, key_entities), max_doc_freq, minimum_should_match floor, and stopword list. If a consumer runs with an existing config file that lacks the new overlap_* fields, a typed config without explicit defaults will either raise a validation error or silently misfire (e.g., zero weights, no stopwords). The plan says these are 'configurable' but doesn't enumerate the fallback defaults that make absence safe.
findings.location: What section — 'weights configurable', 'knobs configurable'; Scope section — 'new overlap_* fields alongside require_plan_review_for_claim/enable_code_review'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section (and mirrored in the AC), list the concrete defaults: e.g., `overlap_field_weights = {problem_keywords: 3.0, component_or_area: 2.5, propositions: 1.0, key_entities: 1.0}`, `overlap_max_doc_freq = 0.7`, `overlap_min_overlap = 1`, `overlap_k = 20`, `overlap_stopwords = []`. Mark all fields `Optional` with these defaults in the typed config so absence is safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected behavior of `related_ticket_ids` when the query ticket has no `parent_id` (e.g., 'a ticket with no parent has no siblings — return empty set for the sibling component').
findings.finding: The plan does not state what 'siblings' means for a ticket with no parent_id (root-level ticket), leaving an edge case in related_ticket_ids undefined.
findings.location: What section — 'siblings (same parent)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'If the query ticket has no parent_id, the sibling set is empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicit default numeric values for each BM25F field weight (e.g., `problem_keywords=2.5`, `component_or_area=2.0`, `propositions=1.0`, `key_entities=1.0`) and for the overlap floor in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies BM25F field weights as 'high' / 'lower' but states no concrete default numeric values for those weights or for the overlap floor, leaving an executor to invent them.
findings.location: What section — 'Field-weighted: problem_keywords and component_or_area high, propositions/key_entities lower (weights configurable)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a defaults table: `problem_keywords: 2.5, component_or_area: 2.0, propositions: 1.0, key_entities: 1.0, overlap_floor: 1, K: 20, max_doc_freq: 0.5` so an executor can act without guessing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'reduced `deps`' with an explicit reference to the actual field name and model (e.g., 'the `relations` field on `TicketDigest` where `relation_type` is any value') so `related_ticket_ids` can be implemented without consulting S1 source.
findings.finding: The term 'reduced deps' (used to define which linked tickets are excluded) is not defined in this plan or cited to any S1/S2 schema.
findings.location: What section — 'linked tickets (from reduced `deps`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the rationale for the bespoke BM25F implementation (vs. `rank-bm25`), the graph-exclusion scope definition, and the `retrieve()` interface contract exposed to S7.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage with a bespoke BM25F implementation and a graph-exclusion pattern, but does not call for any ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture these architectural decisions.
findings.location: ## Scope / What (entire plan)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief `docs/adr/overlap-stage1-bm25f.md` (or a section in CLAUDE.md under a 'Design Decisions' heading) documenting: (1) why `rank-bm25` was rejected in favor of bespoke BM25F (per-field weighting, n<1000, lean ethos), (2) the graph-exclusion boundary (parents/children/siblings/linked), and (3) the `retrieve()` interface contract. Reference it from a docstring in `retrieve.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `retrieve()` logs (at DEBUG or INFO level) the post-exclusion corpus size, the number of candidates returned, and the top score so that a degraded-but-non-erroring run produces an observable signal.
findings.finding: Observability covers only the cold/skip-count and exception paths; there is no specification for instrumenting normal operational behavior (e.g., number of candidates returned, corpus size after exclusion, score distribution), making it difficult to distinguish a degraded-but-non-erroring run from a healthy one.
findings.location: ## What (third bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (last bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: In `retrieve.py`, after scoring: `logger.debug('overlap_retrieve: corpus=%d excluded=%d candidates=%d top_score=%.4f', corpus_size, excluded_count, len(results), results[0].score if results else 0.0)`. Add a corresponding AC line: 'Normal-path log line includes post-exclusion corpus size, candidate count, and top score.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `related_ticket_ids` has ≥2 confirmed (in-scope) call-sites before promoting it to a dedicated `graph.py` module; if S7 is the only other consumer and it's out-of-scope here, inline the graph logic into `retrieve.py` and extract only when S7 actually calls it.
findings.finding: `related_ticket_ids` is a new named helper with exactly one confirmed call-site in this stage (the graph-exclusion filter in `retrieve.py`); its second claimed consumer (S7) is explicitly listed as out-of-scope for this plan, making the shared helper a premature abstraction under the Rule-of-Three.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids`'; Scope section — 'OUT: the LLM judge (S6), review-plan wiring (S7)'
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the parent/ancestor/sibling/linked traversal directly in `retrieve.py` for this stage. When S7 is planned, extract `related_ticket_ids` into `graph.py` at that point — the extraction will be a clean refactor with two confirmed call-sites and a known interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the overlap-specific knobs (field weights, `max_doc_freq`, stopword list, overlap floor, `K`) into a new peer `OverlapConfig` dataclass with its own `[tool.rebar][overlap]` TOML section, consistent with `CompactConfig`, `SyncConfig`, `McpConfig`, etc. in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan adds 7+ new overlap-specific config knobs (field weights for ≥4 digest fields, `max_doc_freq`, stopword list, overlap floor, `K`) directly to `VerifyConfig` rather than a new peer sub-section, inconsistent with the existing pattern (`compact`, `sync`, `mcp`, `reconciler` each get their own dataclass) and inflating a single dataclass that already has 15 fields covering unrelated concerns.
findings.location: Scope section — 'overlap config weights/knobs in `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`, new `overlap_*` fields alongside `require_plan_review_for_claim`/`enable_code_review`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `class OverlapConfig` to `_config_schema.py` with the BM25F knobs, register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES` / `_SECTIONS` under key `'overlap'`, and add `overlap: OverlapConfig = field(default_factory=OverlapConfig)` to `Config`. Reference `config.overlap.k` etc. in `retrieve.py`. This matches every other feature-area config in the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale explaining why `related_ticket_ids` does not delegate the children+deps lookup to the existing cached `build_dep_graph` API, or revise the helper to call `build_dep_graph` for direct children and linked tickets (covering two of the four exclusion categories cheaply via the cache) and add only the ancestor/sibling scan on top.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `related_ticket_ids` as a new helper and explicitly says it 'does NOT refactor next_batch.py', but does not acknowledge that `build_dep_graph` (the existing, cached, public graph API in `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py`) already returns `children` (direct children of the query ticket) and `deps` (all linked tickets) in a single call with caching. A structurally simpler approach would call `build_dep_graph` for children+deps and a single `reduce_all_tickets` for the ancestor+sibling data, avoiding the redundant traversal. The plan provides no rationale for not using `build_dep_graph` here.
findings.location: What section — graph.py helper description; Scope — 'new src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Survey BM25F-capable pure-Python libraries (Whoosh, bm25s) explicitly and document why each was rejected (dependency weight, license, corpus-size assumptions) before committing to a bespoke implementation — or confirm the bespoke is preferred over these alternatives.
findings.finding: The plan dismisses `rank-bm25` for lacking field-weighting and immediately commits to a bespoke BM25F implementation without surveying whether existing BM25F-capable pure-Python libraries (e.g., Whoosh — which natively implements BM25F with per-field weighting; bm25s — which provides fast BM25+ with numpy) fill the gap, and without confirming that those libraries were evaluated and rejected.
findings.location: What section — 'rank-bm25 provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25, but we need per-field weighting; at n<1000 this is trivial and avoids adding a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale per rejected alternative, e.g.: 'Whoosh considered and rejected: adds ~500 KB wheel + index overhead unsuitable for in-memory brute-force over <1000 records. bm25s considered and rejected: numpy transitive dep conflicts with lean-runtime rule. Bespoke 40 LOC is the right trade-off at this corpus size.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the '~40 LOC' estimate against a reference BM25F implementation (including per-field TF normalization, IDF, max_doc_freq pruning, stopword filtering, and overlap floor) and update the estimate or scope statement accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the bespoke BM25F scorer will be '~40 LOC' without verifying this estimate against the actual complexity of a correct BM25F implementation that includes per-field IDF, `max_doc_freq` pruning, a stopword list, a `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor, and configurable field weights — these features collectively represent substantially more than a minimal BM25 scorer.
findings.location: What section — 'bespoke ~40 LOC: rank-bm25 provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
